name: Tane
description: Tāne is a major god in Polynesian mythology, particularly prominent in Māori traditions of New Zealand. He is primarily associated with forests and the natural world, representing the source of light and life. Tāne is known as the creator of humankind in some narratives, fashioning the first human, Hineahuone, from earth. His most celebrated feat is his separation of Ranginui (sky father) and Papatūānuku (earth mother), an act that brought light into the world and allowed life to flourish. This separation is a central motif in Polynesian cosmogony, symbolizing the emergence of order from primordial darkness. Tāne’s influence extends to various aspects of the environment, including birds, trees, and all living things within the forest. He is revered as a guardian and provider, embodying the vital connection between humanity and the natural realm. Different islands and cultures across Polynesia have varying interpretations and names for this deity, reflecting the diverse expressions of their shared ancestral beliefs.
subtitle: God of Forests and Light

keyMyths.title: The Separation of Ranginui and Papatūānuku
keyMyths.description: The most significant myth involving Tāne is his role in separating Ranginui (sky father) and Papatūānuku (earth mother). In the beginning, they were locked in a tight embrace, preventing light from entering the world. Tāne, driven by a desire for light and space for life to flourish, used his immense strength to push them apart. He braced himself against Papatūānuku with his feet and pushed Ranginui upwards with his shoulders, creating the space between earth and sky that allows life to exist. This act is central to the Māori creation narrative.
keyMyths.source: Grey, Sir George. *Polynesian Mythology and Ancient Traditional History of the New Zealand Race.* John Murray, 1854.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Hineahuone
keyMyths.description: After separating Ranginui and Papatūānuku, Tāne sought to populate the world with human beings. He fashioned the first woman, Hineahuone, from earth. He carefully sculpted her form and breathed life into her, creating the first mortal being. This act establishes Tāne as a creator god and highlights the connection between humanity and the earth. Hineahuone becomes the mother of all humankind, solidifying Tāne's role in the genesis of the human race.
keyMyths.source: Orbell, Margaret. *The Concise Encyclopedia of Māori Myth and Legend.* Canterbury University Press, 1998.

keyMyths.title: Tāne and the Search for the Baskets of Knowledge
keyMyths.description: In some traditions, Tāne is also associated with the acquisition of knowledge. He ascended to the heavens to retrieve the three baskets of knowledge (te kete tuauri, te kete tuatea, and te kete aronui) from Io, the supreme being. These baskets contained different forms of knowledge, including ritual, lore, and practical skills. Tāne brought these baskets back to earth, sharing them with humanity and further enriching their understanding of the world. This myth emphasizes Tāne’s role as a bringer of enlightenment and wisdom.
keyMyths.source: Best, Elsdon. *Forest Lore of the Maori.* Dominion Museum, 1942.
